chapter: Foods to Eliminate or Drastically Reduce

claims.claim: 18% increase in mortality for each additional serving consumed
claims.mechanism: Multiple pathways: high empty calories (saturated/trans-fat, sugar, salt), low fibre/vitamins/phytochemicals, carcinogenic molecules (acrylamide, heterocyclic amines, PAHs), controversial additives, and endocrine-disrupting packaging
claims.study_ref: refs-1-3 (two large epidemiological studies, British Medical Journal)

claims.claim: Increased risk of heart disease, stroke, and cancer (especially breast and colon cancer)
claims.mechanism: Trans-fats and saturated fats raise LDL-cholesterol promoting atherosclerosis; carcinogenic molecules damage DNA; low antioxidant content fails to counteract oxidative stress

claims.claim: Drive overconsumption: people eating ultra-processed diet consumed 510 kcal/day more and gained ~1 kg in 14 days
claims.mechanism: Hyper-palatable formulation bypasses satiety signals; despite matched macronutrients, fibre, sugar and sodium, the ultra-processed form drove greater caloric intake
claims.study_ref: ref-7 (NIH randomised clinical trial, 20 participants, crossover design)

claims.claim: Major contributor to pandemic of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease
claims.mechanism: Chronic overconsumption leads to weight gain, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and systemic inflammation

claims.claim: Contain potentially carcinogenic food additives including sodium nitrite, titanium dioxide, talc, and carbon black
claims.mechanism: Sodium nitrite forms carcinogenic nitrosamines; titanium dioxide and others may have direct carcinogenic or endocrine-disrupting effects

claims.claim: Packaging releases carcinogenic and endocrine disruptors such as bisphenol A (BPA)
claims.mechanism: BPA leaches from packaging into food, mimics estrogen, disrupts endocrine signaling, and may promote hormone-sensitive cancers

consumption.reasoning: 60% of calories in average Western diet; even small consumption associated with increased mortality

relationships.description: HFCS is a primary ingredient in ultra-processed foods; combined harm of processing methods plus HFCS amplifies metabolic damage
70-80% of dietary salt comes from processed foods; ultra-processing and excess sodium compound cardiovascular harm
Processed meat products are a category of ultra-processed food with compounded carcinogenic risk from nitrites, smoking, and processing

claims.claim: Heavily implicated in epidemics of obesity, metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes
claims.mechanism: Concentrated fructose causes insulin resistance; fructose is preferentially metabolised in liver leading to de novo lipogenesis and fatty liver; bypasses normal appetite regulation

claims.claim: Fructose causes insulin resistance and fatty liver
claims.mechanism: Fructose is metabolised almost exclusively in the liver via fructokinase pathway, producing acetyl-CoA that drives de novo lipogenesis independent of insulin signaling; chronic exposure causes hepatic insulin resistance

claims.claim: Fructose intake alters intestinal flora, increases gut permeability and causes liver inflammation
claims.mechanism: Disrupts tight junctions in intestinal epithelium increasing permeability; altered microbiome composition; bacterial endotoxins translocate to liver triggering inflammatory cascade

claims.claim: Even moderate consumption of HFCS-containing soft drinks contributes directly to colon cancer development
claims.mechanism: HFCS provides fructose that directly fuels tumour growth in intestinal cells, bypassing normal metabolic regulation

consumption.preparation: N/A - check labels for corn syrup, high-fructose corn syrup, malt syrup, corn sweetener
consumption.reasoning: Cheaper and sweeter than table sugar, hidden in many processed foods and drinks

relationships.description: HFCS is the primary sweetener in most soft drinks; liquid form accelerates absorption and metabolic harm
HFCS is the most harmful form of added sugar due to concentrated fructose content

claims.claim: Increase probability of developing obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, and dental caries
claims.mechanism: Increase calorie intake without contributing essential nutrients; drive insulin resistance; promote chronic inflammation; displace nutrient-dense foods making it impossible to meet vitamin/mineral needs within calorie limits

consumption.amount: Maximum 25g (6 teaspoons) per day for children; none for toddlers under 24 months
consumption.preparation: N/A - check labels for: corn syrup, HFCS, malt syrup, corn sweetener, white/brown sugar, glucose, fructose, dextrose, lactose, maltose, invert sugar, molasses, sucrose, trehalose, turbinado sugar
consumption.reasoning: American Heart Association guideline (ref-8); author recommends no sweets in the home at all

relationships.description: HFCS is the most common and harmful form of added sugar in processed foods
Added sugars are a defining ingredient of ultra-processed foods

claims.claim: Each can consumed by children increases obesity risk by 60%
claims.mechanism: Liquid calories from HFCS bypass satiety mechanisms; rapid fructose absorption drives insulin resistance and de novo lipogenesis

claims.claim: Reducing consumption associated with lower body weight
claims.mechanism: Elimination of liquid empty calories allows normal satiety signaling to regulate intake

claims.claim: Long-term consumption associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, stroke, and cancer (especially pancreatic and colon cancer)
claims.mechanism: Chronic fructose exposure promotes metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, and direct tumour-promoting effects in intestinal tissue

claims.claim: Even moderate HFCS soft drink consumption contributes directly to colon cancer
claims.mechanism: Fructose from HFCS directly fuels tumour growth in intestinal cells

consumption.preparation: N/A - should not be kept in the home
consumption.reasoning: Author: 'soft drinks, candies, and other sweets have no place in our homes'

relationships.description: HFCS is the primary sweetener; soft drink format maximises speed of absorption and metabolic harm
People switching to diet versions still face metabolic risks from artificial sweeteners

claims.claim: Associated with increased risk of obesity and metabolic syndrome despite containing no calories
claims.mechanism: Brain detects discrepancy between sweetness perception and energy intake, triggering a fasting-like state that stimulates hunger and increases food consumption

claims.claim: Sustained sucralose intake stimulates hunger and increases food consumption leading to obesity
claims.mechanism: Specific brain areas detect sweetness-energy mismatch, activating sensory and behavioural responses that force increased calorie consumption

consumption.preparation: N/A - high-intensity sweeteners include aspartame, saccharin, sucralose, acesulfame potassium (Ace-K), Stevia (200-13,000 times sweeter than sugar)
consumption.reasoning: Despite zero calories, evidence suggests metabolic harm comparable to sugar-sweetened versions

relationships.description: Diet versions marketed as healthy alternative but carry similar metabolic risks through different mechanisms
Artificial sweeteners paradoxically increase total calorie consumption by disrupting satiety signaling

claims.claim: Excessive salt increases blood pressure in people with or without hypertension
claims.mechanism: Sodium causes water retention increasing blood volume; stiffens arterial walls; impairs endothelial function; activates sympathetic nervous system

claims.claim: Modest salt reduction lowers systolic/diastolic BP by 5.4/2.8 mmHg in hypertensives and 2.4/1.0 mmHg in normotensives
claims.mechanism: Reduced sodium decreases plasma volume, reduces arterial stiffness, improves endothelial function

claims.claim: Direct relationship between sodium intake and total mortality over 23-26 years, even at lowest intake levels
claims.mechanism: Chronic hypertension from sodium drives atherosclerosis, left ventricular hypertrophy, kidney damage, and stroke

claims.claim: High salt intake promotes inflammation
claims.mechanism: Sodium activates pro-inflammatory Th17 immune cell pathways

claims.claim: Risk of developing hypertension over three decades tightly related to dietary sodium excretion
claims.mechanism: Cumulative sodium-induced vascular damage and remodeling over decades

claims.claim: DASH-Sodium trial confirmed direct relationship between salt intake and blood pressure
claims.mechanism: Dose-response relationship: progressive sodium reduction progressively lowers blood pressure

consumption.amount: Maximum 6g salt (2300mg sodium) per day; adequate intake is 2.3g salt (920mg sodium) per day
consumption.preparation: Use only iodised salt; replace salt with spices and herbs for flavoring; avoid adding salt as condiment (one pinch = ~400mg sodium); 5g iodised salt meets 150mcg daily iodine requirement
consumption.reasoning: WHO and IOM guidelines; 70-80% of salt consumed comes from processed foods, so eliminating processed food is the primary strategy

relationships.description: 70-80% of dietary salt is hidden in processed foods (bread, cheese, processed meats, chips, sauces, cereals); eliminating processed foods is the most effective sodium reduction strategy
Processed meats are very high in salt, compounding cardiovascular risk from saturated fat and carcinogens
Must use iodised salt specifically to meet iodine requirements (150mcg/day) when reducing overall salt intake

claims.claim: Essential for meeting daily iodine requirement (150mcg/day) which is critical for thyroid hormone production
claims.mechanism: Potassium iodide in iodised salt provides iodine substrate for thyroid peroxidase enzyme to synthesize T3 and T4 hormones

claims.claim: Mild iodine deficiency during pregnancy and breastfeeding affects brain development of unborn children and infants, potentially reducing IQ and causing ADHD
claims.mechanism: Thyroid hormones are essential for neuronal migration, myelination, and synaptogenesis in developing brain; deficiency impairs these processes

consumption.amount: ~5g iodised salt per day meets 150mcg iodine requirement; supplement with iodine-rich foods if reducing salt below this
consumption.preparation: Use as sole salt source at home; supplement with dried seaweeds (65-680mcg/100mg), marine seafood (50-100mcg/100mg), small amounts of cheese/milk (80-170mcg/250ml)
consumption.reasoning: Should not eliminate salt entirely; iodine deficiency has serious consequences especially in pregnancy

relationships.description: Must balance salt reduction for cardiovascular health with maintaining adequate iodine intake
Seaweed provides natural iodine allowing further salt reduction while maintaining iodine adequacy

claims.claim: WHO Group 2A carcinogen: probable cause of cancer
claims.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: (1) heme iron promotes N-nitroso compound formation and DNA-damaging oxidative stress; (2) sialic acid incorporation triggers immune/inflammatory reaction with 5-fold increase in carcinoma incidence; (3) high-temperature cooking produces carcinogenic HCAs and PAHs; (4) high protein/branched-chain amino acids trigger cancer-promoting pathways and inhibit immune tumour surveillance

claims.claim: 17% increased colon cancer risk per 100g consumed per day
claims.mechanism: Heme iron promotes carcinogenic N-nitroso compound formation in intestine; oxidative DNA damage from heme iron; sialic acid triggers inflammatory cascade

claims.claim: May increase risk of pancreatic and prostate cancer
claims.mechanism: High protein and branched-chain amino acids activate mTOR and IGF-1 cancer-promoting pathways; inhibit immune cell tumour recognition

claims.claim: Implicated in cardiovascular disease, obesity and type 2 diabetes
claims.mechanism: High saturated fat raises LDL-cholesterol driving atherosclerosis; L-carnitine and choline metabolised by gut bacteria to TMAO which induces inflammation and platelet aggregation

claims.claim: 10-13% higher risk of death associated with increased red meat consumption
claims.mechanism: Combined cardiovascular, cancer, and metabolic disease pathways

claims.claim: L-carnitine and choline in red meat are converted to TMAO by gut bacteria, increasing cardiovascular disease risk by 20%
claims.mechanism: Gut bacteria digest dietary L-carnitine and choline producing trimethylamine, which liver converts to TMAO; TMAO induces inflammation and increases platelet aggregation leading to heart attacks and strokes

claims.claim: Over 95% of human dioxin exposure occurs via meat, eggs, dairy and fatty fish ingestion
claims.mechanism: Dioxins are fat-soluble and bioaccumulate in animal fatty tissue; ingestion transfers these carcinogenic, immunotoxic, endocrine-disrupting compounds to humans

claims.claim: Sialic acid in red meat triggers 5-fold increase in carcinoma incidence
claims.mechanism: Non-human sialic acid (Neu5Gc) incorporates into human tissues, eliciting chronic immune and inflammatory reaction that promotes tumour development

claims.claim: High-temperature cooking (barbecue, charring) produces potent carcinogenic HCAs and PAHs
claims.mechanism: Heterocyclic amines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons form during high-heat cooking; these mutagenic molecules are rapidly absorbed and diffused throughout body (detectable in hair)

consumption.amount: Maximum 350-500g (11-18oz) per week total; ideally small portions a few times per month
consumption.frequency: Drastically reduce; a few times per month maximum
consumption.preparation: Avoid high-temperature cooking/charring; choose ethically sourced, high-quality meat from pasture-raised animals
consumption.reasoning: Ref-42 recommends 350-500g/week max; author suggests even less frequent consumption

relationships.description: Processed red meat combines the harms of red meat with additional carcinogenic processing (nitrites, smoking); even higher cancer risk
Processed meat forms are very high in salt, compounding cardiovascular risk
Red meat is a primary source of saturated fat; combined cholesterol-raising and inflammatory effects

claims.claim: WHO Group 1 carcinogen: strong evidence of causing cancer (especially colon, rectum and gastric cancer)
claims.mechanism: Sodium nitrite curing produces carcinogenic nitrosamines (most broadly acting and potent group of carcinogens in animal experiments); smoking adds PAHs; combined with heme iron, sialic acid and TMAO pathways from the meat itself

claims.claim: 18% increased colon cancer risk per 50g consumed per day
claims.mechanism: Nitrosamine formation from nitrite preservatives; heme iron-driven N-nitroso compound formation; PAHs from smoking process

claims.claim: May increase risk of stomach cancer
claims.mechanism: Nitrosamine formation in stomach from nitrite preservatives; direct mutagenic effect on gastric epithelium

claims.claim: Associated with 10-13% higher risk of death
claims.mechanism: Combined carcinogenic (nitrosamines, PAHs), cardiovascular (saturated fat, TMAO, salt), and metabolic pathways

consumption.preparation: N/A - includes bacon, salami, sausages, hot dogs, processed deli/luncheon meats
consumption.reasoning: WHO Group 1 carcinogen; author recommends very little if any

relationships.description: All harms of red meat plus additional carcinogenic processing; risk is additive
Processed meats are typically very high in salt

claims.claim: Primary dietary determinant of blood cholesterol levels, driving atherosclerosis
claims.mechanism: Saturated fatty acids downregulate hepatic LDL receptors, reducing clearance of LDL-cholesterol from blood; elevated LDL drives plaque formation in arteries causing ~90% of heart attacks, ~60% of strokes, heart failure, peripheral arterial disease and vascular dementia

claims.claim: Naturally occurring trans-fatty acids in milk and red meat have similar adverse effects on blood lipids
claims.mechanism: Trans-fatty acids from ruminant animals affect LDL/HDL ratio similarly to industrial trans-fats

consumption.amount: Drastically reduce; sources include butter, cream, whole milk, cheese, fatty cuts of beef/pork/lamb, lard, chicken skin
consumption.preparation: Replace with monounsaturated (olive oil) and polyunsaturated (nuts, fish) fats
consumption.reasoning: Atherosclerosis is the underlying cause of most cardiovascular death

relationships.description: Red meat is a primary source; saturated fat and TMAO pathways compound cardiovascular risk
Processed foods often contain high levels of saturated fat from palm oil and other cheap fats

claims.claim: Protein restriction or substitution of plant for animal protein markedly inhibits prostate and breast cancer growth
claims.mechanism: Animal protein (rich in sulphur and branched-chain amino acids) activates mTOR and IGF-1 signaling pathways that trigger cancer cell proliferation; also inhibits immune cell capacity to recognise and destroy malignant cells

claims.claim: Moderate protein restriction enhances immune system's capacity to recognise and kill tumour cells and powerfully decreases tumour growth
claims.mechanism: Reduced amino acid supply downregulates mTOR pathway in tumour cells while enhancing T-cell mediated anti-tumour immunity

claims.claim: Excessive animal protein increases rate of ulcerative colitis relapses
claims.mechanism: Gut bacteria digest animal protein producing pro-inflammatory metabolites; casein from milk specifically worsens colitis

claims.claim: L-carnitine and choline from animal protein metabolised to TMAO, increasing cardiovascular disease risk by 20%
claims.mechanism: Gut bacteria convert L-carnitine and choline to trimethylamine; hepatic conversion to TMAO induces inflammation and platelet aggregation independent of traditional risk factors

claims.claim: Casein from milk specifically worsens colitis
claims.mechanism: Casein protein increases intestinal inflammation through pro-inflammatory bacterial metabolite production, contrasting with protective effect of vegetable fibres

consumption.amount: Average American man consumes 90g/day (60%+ animal protein) which is at least 40% higher than needed; reduce substantially
consumption.frequency: Reduce animal protein; substitute plant protein
consumption.preparation: Replace animal protein with legumes, nuts, and other plant sources
consumption.reasoning: Western diet protein intake is ~40% above requirements; high animal protein activates multiple cancer and cardiovascular pathways

relationships.description: Red meat is the primary source of excessive animal protein, L-carnitine, and choline
Processed meat adds carcinogenic processing to the already harmful excess protein load

claims.claim: Unnatural overcrowded conditions, poor food quality and extensive antibiotic use have consequences on quality of meat, milk and eggs
claims.mechanism: Stressed animals produce meat with altered fatty acid profiles; antibiotic growth promoters drive antibiotic-resistant bacteria; contaminated feed concentrates dioxins and pollutants in animal fat

claims.claim: Extensive antibiotic use as growth promoters and infection prevention in factory farming
claims.mechanism: Antibiotics alter animal microbiome and select for antibiotic-resistant bacteria that transfer to humans through meat consumption

consumption.amount: Avoid; choose ethically sourced, pasture-raised animal products in small quantities
consumption.preparation: When consuming animal products, source from farms with natural conditions, no antibiotic growth promoters
consumption.reasoning: Two-thirds of domestic animals raised in factory farms; quality of products is compromised

relationships.description: Factory farming amplifies the inherent health risks of red meat through contamination and quality degradation

claims.claim: Large randomised clinical trials found no protection against chronic diseases from vitamin C, vitamin E and beta-carotene supplementation
claims.mechanism: Isolated high-dose antioxidants lack the synergistic context of whole food matrices; may disrupt redox signaling that the body uses for beneficial purposes (e.g., immune function, apoptosis)

claims.claim: High doses of beta-carotene and vitamin E cause detrimental effects including increased mortality, cancer and haemorrhagic stroke
claims.mechanism: Supraphysiological doses may act as pro-oxidants; high-dose beta-carotene inhibits other carotenoid absorption; high-dose vitamin E may interfere with vitamin K-dependent clotting factors increasing haemorrhagic stroke risk

claims.claim: Supplements cannot replicate the complex balanced array of vitamins, phytochemicals, minerals and oligo-elements in whole foods
claims.mechanism: Synergistic action of nutrients depends on context, concentration ratios found in natural foods, and likely on gut microbiota composition; isolated supplements bypass these interactions

consumption.amount: Avoid unless prescribed by doctor for specific health condition
consumption.preparation: N/A - obtain vitamins and minerals from whole foods instead
consumption.reasoning: Cell and animal studies showed promise but human RCTs consistently showed no benefit or harm from supplementation

relationships.description: People eating poor diets often turn to supplements as a shortcut, but supplements cannot compensate for ultra-processed food consumption
